title: React Rendering Core
description: Core React rendering and state questions that commonly appear in interviews.

cards.question: Why is derived state dangerous in React?
cards.shortAnswer: Because it duplicates a source of truth and creates synchronization bugs.
cards.expectedAnswer: Derived state is dangerous when it stores something that can already be computed from props or existing state. That duplication creates synchronization bugs, makes updates harder to reason about, and adds unnecessary complexity. If a value can be derived cheaply, I prefer computing it instead of storing it.
commonTraps: saying derived state is always wrong
ignoring cases where memoized computation is enough
followUps: When is memoization enough instead of extra state?
What is an example of bad derived state?

cards.question: Why can Context hurt performance in React?
cards.shortAnswer: Because broad context updates can rerender large subtrees.
cards.expectedAnswer: Context is useful, but if a provider value changes often and is consumed widely, React may rerender large parts of the tree. The problem is usually not Context itself, but broad scope and poor boundaries. I try to keep providers narrow, split them by concern, and avoid using Context as a dumping ground for global state.
keyPoints: broad updates can rerender large subtrees
commonTraps: saying Context is bad by default
confusing server state with app-wide UI state
followUps: When would you choose a global store instead?
How would you split a provider by concern?
